site.name: Backurus
site.tagline: Laravel-style API framework for Node.js with a fast developer workflow.
site.footer: Backurus documentation. Build APIs fast with expressive JavaScript.

search.introduction_desc: Framework overview, architecture, CLI, ORM, and workflow.
search.installation_desc: Install dependencies, configure MySQL or SQLite, and boot Backurus.
search.quickstart_desc: Migrate, seed, serve, and call the default API endpoints.
search.routing_desc: Laravel-like route syntax with controller actions and middleware aliases.
search.controllers_desc: Thin controllers, response helpers, and resource orchestration.
search.models_desc: CRUD, query builder, pagination, soft deletes, and relationships.
search.migrations_desc: Schema builder DSL, migration commands, seeders, and status checks.
search.validation_desc: Request validation rules and 422 JSON responses.
search.authentication_desc: JWT login, register, auth middleware, and protected routes.
search.queue_desc: Background jobs, delayed dispatch, queue workers, and scheduler commands.
search.websocket_desc: Realtime notifications and live updates through `/ws`.
search.plugins_desc: Plugins, service container bindings, DB/Cache/Gate facades.

home.title: Backurus brings Laravel-like backend DX to modern JavaScript.
home.lead: Backurus combines `node urus`, expressive routing, an Eloquent-style ORM, schema migrations, JWT auth, queues, scheduler hooks, plugins, facades, and multilingual docs into one clean Node.js framework.
home.panel_body: Backurus is designed for fast REST API development. Its docs are multilingual, searchable, responsive, and consistent with the current framework implementation.
home.card_one_body: Generate controllers, models, requests, policies, resources, modules, migrations, and seeders with `node urus`.
home.card_two_body: Use model CRUD helpers, fluent query builder chains, pagination, soft deletes, and relationships similar to Laravel Eloquent.
home.card_three_body: JWT auth, middleware aliases, queues, scheduler, plugins, WebSockets, facades, and config caching are available out of the box.
home.section_one_body: Backurus keeps the file structure familiar: `app/`, `config/`, `core/`, `database/`, `routes/`, `plugins/`, and `docs/`. Routes auto-load, services are container-driven, and CLI actions follow predictable conventions.
home.section_two_body: Most workflows begin with a generator, followed by migration, seeding, and serving the app. The framework minimizes central wiring so new resources can be shipped quickly.
home.section_three_item_one: REST APIs with route strings like `UserController@index` and middleware aliases like `auth`.
home.section_three_item_two: Database-backed modules with models, request validation, resources, policies, and seed data.
home.section_three_item_three: Realtime and background features through queues, scheduler commands, plugins, facades, and WebSockets.

installation.title: Installation
installation.lead: Install Backurus, configure environment variables, and prepare your database driver before running migrations.
installation.section_one_body: Use Node.js 20 or newer. Backurus currently supports MySQL and SQLite through the built-in database manager.
installation.section_two_body: Run `npm install` from the project root. MySQL projects also require `mysql2`, which is already included in this scaffold.
installation.section_three_body: Set `APP_NAME`, `APP_PORT`, and the database credentials in `.env`. Common keys include `DB_CONNECTION`, `DB_HOST`, `DB_PORT`, `DB_DATABASE`, `DB_USERNAME`, `DB_PASSWORD`, `JWT_SECRET`, and `QUEUE_CONNECTION`.
installation.section_three_item_two: Use `DB_CONNECTION=sqlite` and point `DB_DATABASE` to a local file for SQLite.

quickstart.title: Quick Start
quickstart.lead: The fastest path is install, migrate, seed, and serve.
quickstart.section_one_body: Backurus ships with default `users` and `products` resources, JWT auth endpoints, docs, queue hooks, and route listing support.
quickstart.section_two_item_two: `POST /register` creates a user and emits an event.
quickstart.section_two_item_four: `GET /products` returns the sample resource with pagination metadata.
quickstart.section_three_body: Use `node urus migrate`, `node urus db:seed`, and `node urus serve`. If you need a clean schema rebuild, run `node urus migrate:fresh`.

routing.title: Routing
routing.lead: Backurus routing is intentionally Laravel-like: clean route definitions, controller string actions, and middleware chaining.
routing.section_one_body: Use `Route.get('/users', 'UserController@index')` or `Route.post('/login', 'AuthController@login')`. Routes auto-load from `routes/api.js` and `routes/web.js`.
routing.section_two_body: Chain `.middleware('auth')` to use registered aliases. You can also chain `.name('users.index')` to give the route a consistent identifier for tooling and route listings.
routing.section_three_body: Run `node urus route:list` to inspect every registered route, including method, URI, action, and route name.

controllers.title: Controllers
controllers.lead: Controllers should stay thin and focus on HTTP orchestration.
controllers.section_one_body: Backurus supports instance methods and resolves controller actions from route strings like `ProductController@index`. Read request input, call validation or models, then return response helpers.
controllers.section_three_item_two: Long-running work belongs in jobs or services.
controllers.section_three_item_three: Transformation logic belongs in resources when responses become complex.

models.title: ORM & Models
models.lead: Backurus models expose an Eloquent-style API for CRUD, query builder chains, pagination, timestamps, soft deletes, and relationships.
models.section_one_body: Use `all`, `find`, `create`, `update`, and `delete` on your model classes for common operations.
models.section_four_body: Set `static softDeletes = true` to use `deleted_at`. `created_at` and `updated_at` are filled automatically when `timestamps` is enabled.

migrations.title: Migrations
migrations.lead: Backurus migrations use a schema builder DSL with Laravel-like table methods and modifiers.
migrations.section_two_item_one: `node urus migrate`, `node urus migrate:rollback`, `node urus migrate:reset`.
migrations.section_two_item_three: `node urus db:seed` after schema changes to restore baseline data.
migrations.section_three_body: Backurus supports `nullable()`, `default()`, `unique()`, `index()`, and `unsigned()` on relevant column definitions.

validation.title: Validation
validation.lead: Backurus request validation uses simple rule classes and automatic 422 JSON responses.
validation.section_one_body: Declare rules in request classes with pipe syntax, for example `required|min:3`, `required|email`, or `required|min:6`.
validation.section_three_body: When validation fails, Backurus throws a structured error and the HTTP layer returns a `422` response with detailed field errors.

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.lead: Backurus ships with JWT authentication, login and register endpoints, and an `auth` middleware alias.
authentication.section_one_body: `AuthController@login` signs a JWT, `AuthController@register` creates a user and returns a token, and `/profile` demonstrates protected access.
authentication.section_two_body: Apply `.middleware('auth')` to protected routes. Backurus decodes the bearer token and attaches the authenticated user payload to `req.user`.
authentication.section_three_body: Use the `Gate` facade or route-level guards such as `roleGuard('admin')` when access should be limited by roles or policies.

queue.title: Queue & Schedule
queue.lead: Backurus supports background jobs, delayed dispatch, queue workers, restart signals, and command scheduling.
queue.section_one_body: Jobs are plain classes. Dispatch them with `queue.dispatch(job)` or `queue.dispatchLater(job, delayMs)`.
queue.section_two_body: Use `node urus queue:work` to run workers and `node urus queue:restart` to write a restart signal for long-running workers.
queue.section_three_body: Define scheduled commands in `routes/console.js` with `Schedule.command('route:list').daily()` and run them using `node urus schedule:run`.

websocket.title: WebSocket
websocket.lead: Backurus includes a built-in WebSocket server at `/ws` for realtime notifications and live updates.
websocket.section_one_body: The WebSocket hub attaches to the same HTTP server instance, so APIs and realtime communication share one process.
websocket.section_two_body: Use `ws.emit('notification', data)` from jobs, controllers, or event listeners.
websocket.section_three_body: Use the built-in hub for dashboards, activity feeds, background job notifications, and collaboration features.

plugins.title: Plugins & Facades
plugins.lead: Backurus auto-loads plugins and exposes a small facade layer for common framework services.
plugins.section_one_body: Drop folders into `plugins/` and export a default bootstrap function. Plugins receive access to router, events, queue, container, config, and WebSocket hub.
plugins.section_two_item_two: `Cache.put('key', value)` and `Cache.get('key')` for in-memory cache interactions.
plugins.section_three_body: Use plugins for logging, analytics, and boot-time service registration. Use facades when a terse API improves readability.

cli.title: CLI Reference
cli.lead: Everything in Backurus is driven through `node urus`.
cli.section_three_body: Use generators first, then migrate, seed, inspect routes, and finally serve the app. This gives the fastest Backurus workflow for building a new API module.
